lines: Oldwood hears soft feet and loud hearts. The raiders hear only coins.
Swear the little oath: spare the saplings, mark the stumps, and drive two raiders from the road.
The roadward has tied your blue knot before the waystone. Good; Oldwood likes manners before sword-noise.
Swear the little oath with both signs awake: spare the saplings, mark the stumps, and drive two raiders from the road.
Ah. The waystone has already put moss on your boots. Good; it trusts few readers.
Swear the little oath with your eyes open: spare the saplings, mark the stumps, and drive two raiders from the road.
Two raiders trouble the moss-road. Count them by courage, not by noise: {quest:oldwood-oath.raiders}/2.
The branches are quieter. Good. Carry the oath under the east bough, and the old road will remember you kindly.
East now. Do not bow to the trees; they grow proud enough without encouragement.
The oath sits well on your shoulders. Mind the deeper forest; it has pockets where pages and promises fall out.
A road is a story told by feet. Some feet lie. Yours have not yet.
